sentences.text: [LAUGHS]
Good afternoon.
Sandra ?] Lauterbach.
And I was a survivor.
I was born as Alexandra, which is Olenka, [?
Ola ?] Mayzel.
Yes.
Ola. ?]
I was born November 14, '34 or maybe '35-- [LAUGHS] --because the documents were burned during the war.
So I was close to five years old in September 1st, 1939 when war broke in Poland.
I was born in Warsaw, Poland.
Well, I had a big family.
And I was the only kid.
My mother had two brother.
One was younger, one was older.
But one was married, one-- and in Warsaw-- no children.
The other one was in Palestine, and he wasn't even married.
My father's name was Jerzy-- Jerzy Majzel.
And my mother's name was Halina Lauten-- [LAUGHS]
Well, my father was a lawyer.
He was just graduated from the university when war broke and started his practice.
And he was quite young.
My mother was very wealthy, and-- but she graduated from ballet school.
And she went to a special school in Switzerland when they taught her many languages and everything what a young wealthy girl at the time needed to know-- how to play piano, how to ski.
I had-- my grandfather and grandmother, when war broke, I was staying with them, close to Warsaw.
And there was also my grandfather mother, who was close to 100.
But I was-- I remember that every day I brought a newspaper to my great grandmother.
She was very much alert and up to date.
And I was very vivacious kid.
I liked to dance and to-- to tell poems.
And I was my grandmother-- really-- I was spoiled.
Everybody liked me when war-- you know, at this time, when war broke.
And there were other kids.
I liked to play with them.
I had a bicycle.
And I look at these times as happy times.
Before the-- the war broke, I was usually brought up by different kind of nannies, which weren't always good.
I remember that one of them, I think she was German, she-- [LAUGHS] --no really, because my grandparents were German.
My-- Jews-- they had a lot of possession and buildings in Danzig.
And they lived in-- I remember, as a little girl, I was going to the ocean, Baltic.
And my mother was going to the ocean.
And I was crying that she will disappear.
And so I think that one of my nannies were really beating me up.
And I was too small to explain what happened to me.
[LAUGHS] Because she put her fingers to my throat so I couldn't scream when she was beating me up.
And then I had sore throat.
And everybody was telling me that I have a cold.
And I couldn't explain, because I didn't talk yet so well-- [LAUGHS] --what really happened.
It was too complicated.
So because of the war time, I was with my grandmother, who was really so good to me.
Unfortunately, or fortunately, I didn't even know what Jewish means.
Well, I was a little girl.
And I know that my father was a Polish officer, and he will beat Germans.
There was very clean Polish language used at home.
And Yiddish-- Jewish-- was considered not-- not elegant language.
And I didn't have any Jewish background.
When war broke, I was told I am Jewish, because Jews, adults, were wearing David Star on their arm.
And I remember running, trying to prove to my friends that I am not Jewish.
Because I didn't like it.
And I was running fast to-- to show that I will go first to the person who didn't have the Star of David on the arm.
It was kind of like being different, finding out that I'm Jewish.
And my father was, I think, fighting atheist.
He didn't-- he was not religious at all.
My-- his father was a president of Jewish community.
I think it was for Warsaw or for whole Poland.
I don't know.
I don't remember him very well.
I remember well other members of my family.
My birthday parties and other birthday parties.
And I read in my mother's letters, you know, that I was performing a lot in-- on these parties.
And that it was hilarious.
She was laughing so hard-- [LAUGHS] --looking at me when I was singing and dancing.
And you know, I-- I took it very seriously, but it looked hilarious.
I was small.
So yes, I remember parties, but not really, just birthday parties.
Yes, I remember having friends, other kids.
I have even pictures which are-- which were-- were with some friends on the block.
And I think I was the smallest one.
[LAUGHS]
No!
No.
I was coming to be-- I was four year old, so I-- I didn't-- I wasn't at the age.
I think I-- mostly it was-- I liked to be moving.
I-- I was with my grandmother and grandfather.
My mother was very often going to Warsaw, and we were living close to Warsaw.
So I was just up, and going out, and playing with other kids, and singing and dancing, and--
--riding the bike.
[LAUGHS]
Yes, I do.
Yeah, I remember, this was funny, because I was playing the-- the play for their dolls, and I was a gypsy woman.
And I was running after another woman who was unhappily-- she lost some man.
And he cheated on her.
And I was as a experienced gypsy woman-- was telling her that, don't believe men.
Because they all leave.
They are like dogs.
If they are dogs-- home dogs, or wild dogs-- they are just dogs.
And-- [LAUGHS] --whatever.
You know, like gypsies in Poland were telling you fortune or were supposed to be--
--smart.
Well, I was really in upper middle class.
That what they called Jewish aristocrats, where-- where the few generations with money.
And they had a lot of money, my-- particularly my grandparents, and high education, so--
Both-- both grandparents were wealthy, well situated, educated-- even my great grandmother.
And they were assimilated, too.
I mean, they knew they were Jews.
They married between each other.

But they-- they really-- I-- I don't think they were religious Jews or that they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --my-- my-- one of my uncle, younger brother of my mother, was studying in Munich, in Germany.
He was studying law.
And he went to Palestine, as I mentioned, in '35.
The older brother, also-- both-- both my uncle went to German school.
And my mother spoke German like-- like Germans.
They knew poetry.
[LAUGHS] My uncle sometimes lives-- he lives there, in German.
So we say that he speaks and writes so beautifully.

I guess at this level, Jewish people, like my father, wanted to-- probably spoke better Polish than average Pole, and tried to show that he's braver, because Jews were considered cowards than-- than Polish men.
And, of course, when he went to an army, he got some medal, because he was brave, you know-- [LAUGHS]
I read about it.
I heard about it.
I, you know.
I remember some funny rhymes about-- children were saying about the Jews, which were ugly, you know, like--
Yeah.
In Polish?
[LAUGHS]
No.
You don't understand Polish.
It was some really-- a-- well, little Jewish go-- go here.
I cannot go, because it's Sabbath, and it-- it was something-- something which you wouldn't like to identify with.
Or like some Jewish woman-- it's-- it was just for rhyme.
It's making-- like putting her saliva some place.
[LAUGHS] It was stupid.
But it wasn't pleasant.
Oh, yes.
I remember bombs.
I remember I was going to underground and hiding, because bombs were going around.
Well, my life really changed.
It didn't change drastically.
My father was, as I say, away.
It started that-- the knowledge that I am Jewish and going to Warsaw ghetto wasn't so bad.
At the beginning of the ghetto, we had pretty nice, I think, in what I remember, apartment on [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is the street, [NON-ENGLISH] 32.
And my grandmother was cleaning the place and making it pleasant.
And my grandfather was there.
And he trying to-- I think he was selling his paintings or try to get some money.
And my mother was working.
There was a little cafe in the ghetto.
And she was a waitress, which all these, you know, young, educated women were doing.
And I was still playing with kids.
And when I was getting older, they gave me to very well known orphanage, a place to go and play with children there.
Because they thought that the children were brought up well.
I also found that in my mother's letter that they didn't want me to have to be with children who don't have a good background.
And in an orphanage home, I will meet better kind of kids.
There was a pretty well known pedagogist, Dr.
Korczak.
There were many books and big literature about him.
And--
Oh, well I don't-- it was smaller.
It was-- it was on the sixth floor.
And-- and there was no elevator.
And I have to-- to my grandmother, we all have to-- to go all this floor.
And I remember that I always were waiting-- [LAUGHS] --till last moment when I needed to pee.
And then it was really hard to make it to sixth floor.
But the beginning of ghetto wasn't even so bad.
The-- my real terrible memories start in '42, July '42.
Yeah?
It was unpleasant, because some people were really very hungry in this ghetto.
And I remember once I was going on the street with piece of bread in my hand.
And a big man-- and there was nobody on the street.
A big man just tried to get this bread from me.
He wanted to-- to take it.
He was hungry.
And I didn't know.
I was so frightened or shocked that instead of giving him the bread-- I wasn't hungry at the time-- I started to scream really very loud and not giving it to him.
And people were coming from, you know, from place.
And then my mother was very cautious that I wouldn't walk by myself on the street.
But at this time, we weren't so hungry.
And I was pretty clean.
I-- I-- [SIGHS] --remember that I-- there was a typhus.
The-- that is a disease which is brought up by lice.
Typhus.
And I know that my mother was able to give me a vaccination made out of lice to my body so to protect me.
And unfortunately, I got very sick after this vaccination.
And I remember that when I-- I-- I-- I was unconscious from fever.
And my grandmother, didn't-- was all the time, whenever I was a little bit conscious or opened my eyes, I saw my grandmother.
And she always was telling me so interesting stories, fairy tale.
I loved her a lot.
I really loved my grandmother.
We were very close.
My mother's mother.
And also--
Rutka.
Ruta but babica-- I mean, grandma.
And my other-- my father's mother was more kind of serious.
She wanted me to learn how to write, how to draw.
She thought-- I was, I think, a little hyperactive kid.
And my mother's mother was really telling me that I am the most beautiful-- [LAUGHS] --girl.
And my future will be wonderful.
And she was telling me poems, which sometimes made me cry.
And she was just-- she loved to make me dresses, on-- knitting it, or anything for me.
[LAUGHS]
So that wasn't so bad, even though I was in ghetto.
How many people lived in your house?
In this-- at-- when the ghetto started, we just lived-- our family alone in this place, in Warsaw, on [NON-ENGLISH].
And I was going to orphanage.
As I say, everything changed in to-- first, my-- my grandfather got 'tih-fus'?
Typhus.
And when he was very sick, I had to change my location and live with my father's mother, with my paternal grandmother.
And then my grandfather died.
And I returned home.
And my [SIGHS] grandmother jumped from sixth floor.

At first, she took me at night to her bed and was really, really, very, very-- giving me more time than usual at telling me stories and kissing me, and telling me that I will be happy when I will be big.
And I will be living in places where there will be elevators, which go so fast, and some things which I felt were wonderful.
And next morning, when I woke up, I heard my mother crying.
And I heard people were in our apartment.
And I was lying there and heard that-- I was shocked that my mother was crying and that people were saying that my grandmother, at night had jumped from sixth floor to kill herself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think that when-- that was 22nd of July, 1942.
And since then, all my bad memories started of war, real war.
Well, my mother came to me and told me that my-- that my grandmother left town, because many Jewish people in the ghetto were leaving town for concentration camp.
But they didn't know that they were concentration camp.
They were told that they were going to work.
So that just my mother-- my grandmother left.
And she helped me to-- to get dressed quickly.
And then we never returned to this apartment.
And we were really homeless.
We were living one day here, another day some other place.
The orphanage place was taken to the concentration camp.
And I never tried to tell my mother that I know that my grandmother died.
And I was looking down, you know-- always wonder how a person can jump from such a height and kill herself, that it-- it must be awful.
But there were no other way to kill.
There were no pills, or gas, or other things which she could do.
And it-- after we were homeless, it was like one day I remember that the Germans put us into a big line of people and were giving some numbers.
And numbers of-- when you get, I think, other number you were supposed to go out and be free.
And when you got another number, you were going to concentration camp, to Umschlagplatz first.
And we--
It's a place where-- it's like a terminal or rail station where train are taking-- were taking people to Treblinka.
That's what concentration camp was for Warsaw ghetto.
So we were not lucky.
We got a bad number, my mother.
And I remember getting to some house where there were a lot of dead people, close to Umschlagplatz.
And there was a lot of-- excuse me-- shit, because people didn't have place, or bathroom, or whatever.
And we didn't have any food with us.
Now who was-- that you were there with your mother?
With my mother.
Plenty of people.
Not-- not family-- just strangers-- or family, maybe too, I-- but not with us.
There were plenty of people on this Umschlagplatz.
And there was a outside.
And I remember staying with the group of people.
And there was-- in ghetto, there were policeman, Jewish policeman.
And next to my mother was standing a woman with a little kid.
And my mother had just two-- two pieces of sugar with her.
And she gave one piece to me and one piece to this other kid.
She didn't take it for herself.
And this other woman and my mother became friends.
And she put us on the side where this policeman wives were standing.
So we were a little bit longer there.
But it was very frightening experience to be-- for me.
It was one of the worst-- it was the worst experience to be on Warsaw, Umschlagplatz.
We were there about 10 days.
There was no food.
There were people killed all the time.
The nights were very cold.
And I was so hungry and so weak that I slept.
I was just-- there was no bath, of course, but I was just, you know, on my feet and try-- with closed eyes, and didn't have any strength.
But I was very terrified, because the people were killed, or mothers were trying to escape, leaving her dead children behind, and putting men's clothes.
Because that's the way to-- to-- that they could make it.
I did see it.
And I was very scared.
I-- it stays with me whenever I hear the whistle of a train.
It connects with me, with this experience of night at the Umschlagplatz, in Warsaw ghetto.
And it was just a miracle, a real miracle, that my mother and I get out.
It was just because she was a wife of Polish officer.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH], in reserve.
And also, some woman gave her white, like a-- a nurse's, coat.
And we were able to-- to leave after 10 days.
OK.
